video_title: Navigating a Market Moving at Game Speed | Portfolio Manager Roundtable
gist: Hedgeye PMs Keith McCullough, Brooks Kubryk, John McNamara and RPK argue the June 2026 index-rebalance season is mechanically forcing Mag 7 underperformance as fund managers sell even slices of mega-caps to fund additions like Marvell, and that surviving a 'game moving at game speed' near a generational top requires systematic, risk-managed, short-duration process rather than thematic buy-and-hold.

insights.claim: Mag 7 underperformance in June 2026 is partly mechanical, not just fundamental, because index funds must sell an even slice of every holding to fund new additions, disproportionately hitting the heaviest weights like Nvidia, Apple and Microsoft.
insights.speaker: Brooks Kubryk
insights.so_what: Treat June mega-cap weakness as flow-driven and potentially temporary rather than a fundamental break; don't bottom-fish until the rebalance overhang clears.
insights.quote: this this um kind of noted underperformance of Mag 7 is is mechanical in in my estimation on top of what's going on with the the fundamental inputs

insights.claim: The June index-rebalance calendar runs from MSCI at end of May into the S&P Dow Jones and Nasdaq quarterly rebalance, then Russell on Friday, with Nasdaq expected to add SpaceX via an ad hoc fast-track on the evening of July 6th.
insights.speaker: Brooks Kubryk
insights.so_what: Maps the specific dates of forced flows so traders can anticipate mechanical buying/selling pressure and the SpaceX add as a discrete catalyst.
insights.quote: I believe it's now the evening of July 6th is the day when Nasdaq is expected to add SpaceX in its ad hoc super fast track

insights.claim: Campbell Soup and Pool Corporation (POOL) were removed from the S&P 500 in the June quarterly rebalance because both are clearly below the roughly $22 billion inclusion floor and under $10 billion in size.
insights.speaker: Brooks Kubryk
insights.so_what: Removals create forced-selling targets while the additions replacing them get forced buying; the ~$22B floor is a screen for predicting future deletions.
insights.quote: The The floor is around 22 billion these days, and both those companies are are clearly below 10.

insights.claim: Replacing a deleted name with a roughly $250 billion company like Marvell forces index funds, which must stay fully invested, to raise cash by selling proportionally across all holdings, magnifying the hit to the largest weights.
insights.speaker: Brooks Kubryk
insights.so_what: The bigger the addition relative to the deletion, the larger the forced outflow from mega-caps; size the addition to gauge the selling pressure.
insights.quote: the way that you do that is by selling an even slice of everything that you have in that fund

insights.claim: The same rebalance dynamic will be even more pronounced in the Nasdaq 100 (the Qs) late next week because the index has fewer names to absorb the forced selling, concentrating the impact on the top names.
insights.speaker: Brooks Kubryk
insights.so_what: Expect sharper mechanical pressure on Nasdaq mega-caps than on S&P names during the Qs rebalance window.
insights.quote: there are even less names to exert this force onto. So, it should be even more pronounced on the the top names.

insights.claim: Much of the rebalance pressure comes not from indexers transacting on the close but from traders positioning ahead of the known flows, so picking bottoms during the overhang means fighting the tide for another week or two.
insights.speaker: Brooks Kubryk
insights.so_what: Wait for the rebalance overhang to clear before timing entries in pressured mega-caps rather than catching the falling knife.
insights.quote: It It is You're fighting the tide if you're you're trying to pick bottoms

insights.claim: Index rebalancing is a near-permanent return stream because additions and deletions, mergers and sunsetting companies happen almost every quarter; the only quarter with no S&P changes was last June, which observers initially mistook for a mechanical failure.
insights.speaker: Brooks Kubryk
insights.so_what: Frames rebalance arbitrage as a recurring, structurally reliable strategy rather than an occasional event.
insights.quote: The only quarter I know of where they did absolutely nothing was last June, and people were stuck to their screens thinking it was a mechanical failure for like 3 hours.

insights.claim: Campbell Soup was both a fundamental short and an amplified one as accelerating volume on declining price signaled traders front-running its index deletion and Marvell's addition.
insights.speaker: Keith McCullough
insights.so_what: Watch accelerating down-volume in sub-floor S&P names as a tell for imminent index deletion and a short opportunity.
insights.quote: the volume started to go up as the stock went down, and it just kept going down, down, down on accelerating volume

insights.claim: The most severe short-side pain comes on days when a hedge fund pod is being liquidated, because deleveraging is mechanical, done in a day or two, and unscrupulous in how fast positions are taken down.
insights.speaker: Brooks Kubryk
insights.so_what: Crowded short names can violently squeeze when a pod blows up; recognize forced-liquidation price action to avoid being on the wrong side.
insights.quote: When you find a pod that's getting let go from a hedge fund, they they are unscrupulous when when they take the positions down. It's like it's done in a day or two.

insights.claim: Crowded shorts are identifiable from bi-weekly short-interest data, elevated put skew and non-discretionary price action, making the short side structurally crowded and tricky to trade.
insights.speaker: Brooks Kubryk
insights.so_what: Use short-interest, put skew and price-action tells to gauge crowding before taking or holding short positions.
insights.quote: We get data every 2 weeks from from short interest. You get you get put skew. You you can recognize what's going on with the price action.

insights.claim: On the prior day Goldman Sachs's high-momentum basket (longs minus shorts) fell over 6%, roughly four times the S&P 500's move, marking where both the pain and the gains have concentrated.
insights.speaker: Keith McCullough
insights.so_what: Momentum-factor dispersion is running ~4x the index, so factor positioning dominates single-name P&L right now.
insights.quote: the longs minus the shorts, which is called the high momentum basket by Goldman Sachs, was down like over 6% on the day, which was down like you know, four times the S&P 500

insights.claim: Prime brokers like Goldman build a basket for everything and use prime-brokerage data to tag positioning, so a trader who can't identify the sucker at the table is the sucker.
insights.speaker: Keith McCullough
insights.so_what: Assume your crowded positioning is visible to dealers and other pods; trade as if your hand is known.
insights.quote: if you don't know who the sucker is at the table, it's you

insights.claim: On the day of recording the top-performing factor in the market was short interest, with days-to-cover up about 190 basis points.
insights.speaker: Keith McCullough
insights.so_what: A short-interest squeeze leading the tape signals a difficult environment for short books and supports staying long momentum.
insights.quote: the top performing factor in the market today is short interest days to cover is up about 190 basis points

themes.theme: Process evolution and systematic risk management

insights.claim: RPK overhauled his long/short strategy in three steps, the most impactful in the second half of May, adding a cash buffer to actively manage shorts, respecting Hedgeye trade lines for sizing, and using the timing process to govern portfolio extension to control volatility.
insights.speaker: RPK
insights.so_what: The strategy's post-May performance reflects a deliberate volatility-control redesign, useful context for evaluating the ETF's recent track record.
insights.quote: you can't fire and forget with shorts, right? They have to be constantly managed.

insights.claim: RPK's performance since May is materially different from the drawdown he had going into it, with the redesigned, more systematic process driving the improvement.
insights.speaker: Keith McCullough
insights.so_what: Recent inflection in the strategy's returns is attributed to process change, not luck, when assessing the product.
insights.quote: if you look at the performance since May, it's very different than the performance drawdown that you had coming into that

insights.claim: Respecting trade lines drove RPK to exit an overweight energy position quickly when oil broke its trade lines, a counterintuitive move that proved additive to returns.
insights.speaker: RPK
insights.so_what: Demonstrates a rules-based exit discipline: break of immediate-term trade line triggers exit regardless of conviction or thesis.
insights.quote: as soon as these things broke trade lines, I was just gone

insights.claim: Hedgeye holds risk management and avoiding drawdowns and crashes as its core differentiator, arguing no household-name asset-management product is built around risk management the way an insurance policy is built around protecting a home.
insights.speaker: Keith McCullough
insights.so_what: Positions Hedgeye's edge as drawdown control versus Bogle's low-fee edge; the pitch is downside protection, not just alpha.
insights.quote: Bogle came up with one discerning difference... Lower fees. Hedgeye came up with this very discerning difference... which is called risk management.

insights.claim: John McNamara's S&P 500 hedge product is fully systematic, driven by Hedgeye's risk ranges with no discretionary or emotional input, distinguishing it from competitors.
insights.speaker: John McNamara
insights.so_what: A rules-based hedge removes timing emotion; the appeal is consistency of the hedging signal rather than discretionary calls.
insights.quote: what I do is also totally systematic and doesn't have any feelings or emotions

insights.claim: Holding a portfolio of strategies that all perform identically would be alarming from a risk perspective, so dispersion in returns across Hedgeye's strategies is by design.
insights.speaker: RPK
insights.so_what: Frames divergent performance across the strategy suite as healthy diversification, not inconsistency.
insights.quote: I would actually be alarmed if they were all performing exactly the same way

themes.theme: Active vs passive and the broken old wall

insights.claim: Passive products have captured 90-plus percent of total ETF capitalization, and most so-called active ETFs are crap, with even levered ETFs being indexes with leverage rather than genuinely active.
insights.speaker: Keith McCullough
insights.so_what: The active share of ETFs is small and low-quality, framing a large opportunity for genuinely process-driven active products.
insights.quote: The reason why passive has taken, what is it, 90 plus percent of the ETF total cap right now

insights.claim: Many thematic active ETFs are effectively levered concentrated bets on one or a handful of stocks that look great while the theme works but have no risk-management process when it turns, so they just wait for the theme to recover.
insights.speaker: John McNamara
insights.so_what: Treat thematic active ETFs as unhedged levered theme bets; their lack of drawdown control is the key risk to screen for.
insights.quote: when the thematic doesn't work there's no risk management procedure in place and there's no real process

insights.claim: Durable successful active funds tend to be spawned from established research houses with real process and people, whereas 'party trick' active funds come from a loose understanding of how something works and aren't thoughtful products.
insights.speaker: Brooks Kubryk
insights.so_what: Use research-house pedigree and process depth as a filter when selecting active funds likely to have staying power.
insights.quote: a hedge fund is a trick that rich people play on other rich people

insights.claim: The old-wall index-rebalance edge was closely guarded by a handful of desks for decades until the 2020 Tesla S&P inclusion exposed it publicly, triggering a proliferation of rebalance pods at hedge funds.
insights.speaker: Brooks Kubryk
insights.so_what: The 2020 Tesla add democratized rebalance-arb knowledge, increasing crowding in the strategy and compressing its returns.
insights.quote: what happened after 2020 with the the big Tesla trade is that the the whole world saw what what index reval was

insights.claim: Brooks Kubryk argues the index-rebalance return stream, previously available only to those with basket-trading technology or custom baskets, is ideally suited to the ETF wrapper, where it had not previously existed.
insights.speaker: Brooks Kubryk
insights.so_what: Identifies a structural product gap now being filled, the investment case for the rebalance ETF.
insights.quote: the ETF is the absolute perfect wrapper to do something like this

insights.claim: The ETF wrapper offers transparency, accountability and lower fees than a hedge fund because positions must be published at the end of every day, run by the same people who ran the hedge funds.
insights.speaker: Keith McCullough
insights.so_what: Frames the ETF as a cheaper, fully transparent route to hedge-fund-style strategies, the core sales argument.
insights.quote: the multiple factors that are beautiful in terms of that structure including lower fees than what a hedge fund charges by the same guys who ran the hedge funds

insights.claim: Roughly 140 companies in the S&P 500 would not be included if the index started from scratch today, yet those 140 names represent only about 3% of every invested dollar.
insights.speaker: Brooks Kubryk
insights.so_what: Quantifies how top-heavy the index is; owning the S&P is far less diversified than the 500-name count implies.
insights.quote: those 140 are only 3% of every dollar you have invested

insights.claim: The claim that S&P 500 investors are diversified across 500 names is factually correct but kind of dishonest given the extreme weight concentration in the top names.
insights.speaker: Brooks Kubryk
insights.so_what: Reframes passive indexing as a concentrated mega-cap bet, relevant for anyone relying on the index for diversification.
insights.quote: you're diversified across 500 names is like it's factually correct, but it's kind of dishonest

insights.claim: Hedgeye sees the current market as the third generational bubble of McCullough and RPK's careers, comparable to 1999-2000 and the 2007 peak into 2008, with crypto's 2021 episode treated as outright fraud rather than a stock bubble.
insights.speaker: Keith McCullough
insights.so_what: Positions current conditions as a late-stage generational top, framing risk-management priority and eventual top-calling.
insights.quote: running money into and out of the mother of all bubbles

insights.claim: Zero-days-to-expiration options can be 70% or more of daily options flow, with daily options concentrated in former Mag 7 names like Tesla, embedding extreme short-termism into market structure.
insights.speaker: Keith McCullough
insights.so_what: 0DTE dominance means immediate-term price momentum drives flows, favoring fast, short-duration process over buy-and-hold.
insights.quote: zero days to expiration options can be upwards of 70% or more of daily options flow

insights.claim: Hedgeye defines 'trades' as immediate-term durations of three weeks or less, chosen because the entire hedge fund universe and the machine operate off one-month price momentum as a factor exposure, so getting ahead of that window is the edge.
insights.speaker: Keith McCullough
insights.so_what: Trading inside the 3-week window front-runs the one-month momentum factor that dominates institutional flows.
insights.quote: the entire hedge fund universe operates and the machine at large operates off 1 month price momentum as a factor exposure. So, you should probably front run that.

insights.claim: Since Hedgeye began publishing risk ranges in 2015, the S&P 500 has closed within its daily published risk range about 83% of the time.
insights.speaker: John McNamara
insights.so_what: An 83% hit rate underpins the systematic S&P hedge product's signal reliability.
insights.quote: since you guys started publishing risk ranges back in 2015, the S&P 500 has closed within its daily published risk range 83% of the time

insights.claim: Nasdaq volatility (the VXN) moved into the 30s on the prior day and is now trending, which Hedgeye reads as the pace of the game picking up via the rate of change of the rate of change.
insights.speaker: Keith McCullough
insights.so_what: A trending VXN in the 30s signals accelerating, potentially toppy conditions and warrants faster, more defensive positioning.
insights.quote: when like Nasdaq volatility did yesterday, when it went into the 30s, that is the definition of the pace of the game picking up

insights.claim: Hedgeye's quad map flags a July quad four with downside volatility breakouts that have been episodic and non-trending, while the VXN breakout is now trending into that regime.
insights.speaker: Keith McCullough
insights.so_what: A trending volatility breakout into a July quad four signals rising odds of a sustained drawdown regime.
insights.quote: our our quad map would say during a full-blown quarterly quad four. Uh we have a July quad four that's had plenty of fits and starts of downside volatility breakouts.

insights.claim: McCullough argues you should own assets already in motion rather than hoping motion will come, since being long negative momentum spots the market several hundred basis points a year before even trying to beat it.
insights.speaker: RPK
insights.so_what: Quantifies the structural handicap of contrarian value positioning and supports a momentum-following bias.
insights.quote: being long negative momentum is like spotting the market several hundred basis points every year and then trying to beat it

insights.claim: Warren Buffett's long-term alpha was generated by fewer than 50 single stocks, well over half of which were bought when the S&P traded between seven and twelve times earnings, conditions investors cannot replicate today.
insights.speaker: Keith McCullough
insights.so_what: Debunks Buffett as a replicable template, justifying short-duration, process-driven strategies for managers without his structural advantages.
insights.quote: well over half of those names were when the S&P was trading between seven and 12 times earnings

insights.claim: Buffett's model is not replicable for most asset managers because Berkshire's attached insurance company provides captive monthly inflows and an effectively negative cost of capital, unlike funds that face outflows after a few months of underperformance.
insights.speaker: RPK
insights.so_what: Captive permanent capital, not stock-picking genius, is the structural edge; daily-marked funds cannot copy it.
insights.quote: he basically has almost like a negative cost of capital

insights.claim: Private markets have become popular largely because they avoid reporting daily volatility, effectively running a Buffett-style model where P&L is reported infrequently and can be skewed by those incentivized to skew it.
insights.speaker: John McNamara
insights.so_what: Frames private-market appeal as volatility concealment rather than superior returns, a lens for evaluating private allocations.
insights.quote: basically you have a Buffett model. It's just you don't report P&L on a daily basis

insights.claim: RPK believes that applying his current fast, systematic tools over the last ten years would have produced a materially better track record by cutting losses quicker, not giving ideas as much rope, and letting winners run with process.
insights.speaker: RPK
insights.so_what: Argues disciplined fast process beats slower discretionary investing, the case for adopting systematic timing tools.
insights.quote: If I was using a lot of tools I'm using right now, running money even the last 10 years, like I would have had a much better track record even than I had.

insights.claim: Citing Paul Tudor Jones, McCullough says the hardest part of a move to get right is the last third, because valuation bears and winner-sellers come in too early near generational tops.
insights.speaker: Keith McCullough
insights.so_what: Warns against exiting winners too early into a top and stresses riding momentum until trade lines break.
insights.quote: Paul Tudor Jones who'd say the hardest part of the move to get right is the last third of it

insights.claim: Hedgeye is considering launching a Bitcoin ETF that would not be a levered-long version of Bitcoin, signaling a risk-managed rather than directional crypto product.
insights.speaker: Keith McCullough
insights.so_what: Signals a forthcoming differentiated Bitcoin product from Hedgeye that avoids simple levered-long exposure.
insights.quote: we're thinking about launching an ETF on that front which wouldn't be your levered long version of a Bitcoin let's just start with that

insights.claim: John McNamara's systematic S&P 500 hedge competes against a roughly $30 billion JP Morgan product that hedges only quarterly, giving the nimbler active hedge an information and timing advantage.
insights.speaker: Keith McCullough
insights.so_what: Positions the daily-managed hedge against a large quarterly-only incumbent, the competitive differentiation pitch.
insights.quote: There's a $30 billion product at JP Morgan that John competes with. That... only hedges everything that you see going on in your world quarterly.

insights.claim: Hedgeye Asset Management has operated just over a year under its own banner, launching multiple ETF strategies, with Sam Ryburn's S&P 500-benchmarked ETF being the only one that directly competes against the SPY.
insights.speaker: Keith McCullough
insights.so_what: Clarifies the product lineup and that only one strategy is benchmarked to beat SPY, the rest being tools.
insights.quote: the only uh ETF and strategy that we've launched that uses the S&P 500 as his benchmark
